does: This paper identifies a fundamental incompatibility in reward-guided text generation (RGTG) methods that score partial sequences using reward models trained on full sequences. The authors propose training a Bradley-Terry reward model explicitly on partial sequences and develop an autoregressive sampling procedure that generates text according to an implied tokenwise policy. They provide theoretical analysis showing that the resulting policy can be expressed as a ratio of two distinct RLHF policies, establishing a principled connection between token-level guidance and full-sequence preference learning. The work includes empirical studies examining the properties of these reward models and the quality of generations they produce.
connects: This work builds on recent research in prediction-time steering for language models that seeks to avoid expensive fine-tuning. It responds to the heuristic motivation underlying existing reward-guided generation methods, which apply sequence-level reward models to score incomplete sequences during decoding without formal justification. The paper connects to classical work on Bradley-Terry models and preference learning, adapting these frameworks to the sequential generation setting. It also engages with the broader RLHF literature, grounding tokenwise decoding in the theory of policy learning and showing how partial-sequence rewards relate to the full-policy objectives used in standard alignment approaches.
enables: This work establishes theoretical foundations for more principled approaches to lightweight, test-time steering of language model generation. By clarifying the relationship between partial-sequence rewards and full-sequence policies, it enables better design of efficient alignment methods that don't require model fine-tuning. The theoretical framework could facilitate development of improved decoding algorithms that properly account for the sequential nature of text generation. Future work could leverage these insights to design better reward models for specific applications, explore hybrid approaches combining partial-sequence and full-sequence feedback, or extend the framework to other structured generation tasks where token-level guidance is valuable.
